personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Harry is brave, reactive, emotionally volatile, and stubborn. He is defined by a tension between self-control and explosive anger — he spends weeks suppressing rage at Aunt Marge through gritted-teeth calculation, then loses control entirely when she insults his father. He is curious and proactive (sneaking to Hogsmeade, confronting Lupin about the boggart), self-conscious about weakness (shame over fainting near dementors, hiding tears after hearing his father's voice), and capable of dry humor and sarcasm. His voice is direct, short-sentenced, and emotionally raw when pushed. He is also quietly strategic — the negotiation with Uncle Vernon over the Hogsmeade form shows cold-blooded leverage. He is not passive: he initiates, challenges, and acts.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves Harry's core traits well. His bravery is intact (volunteering for Buckbeak, confronting Black, casting the Patronus). His emotional volatility is present — he snaps at Marge, storms out, and declares he will kill Black. His self-consciousness about the dementors is shown ('I'm scared, Professor'). His dry humor appears in small moments (the Firebolt 'defective' joke, the Pepper Imp scene). His directness and short, blunt speech patterns are preserved. The Shrieking Shack confrontation captures his rage and his ultimate restraint.
personality_voice.difference: Several secondary traits are compressed or lost. The book's extended portrait of Harry's strategic self-control — weeks of suppressing anger at Marge, the calculated leverage over Vernon — is reduced to a single dinner scene. His intellectual curiosity (doing homework by flashlight, researching death omens, noticing inconsistencies in Fudge's behavior) is largely absent. His shame and private grief over hearing his mother's voice is present in the film but less textured — the book shows him crying and hiding it, lying awake replaying the screams. The film Harry's declaration 'I'm going to kill him' is more dramatically blunt than the book's more conflicted, unresolved internal state ('he didn't know what he wanted to do'). The film slightly simplifies his emotional complexity into cleaner beats.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits — bravery, volatility, stubbornness, self-consciousness, dry humor, directness — are preserved. The penalty is for the loss of secondary texture: the strategic coldness, the intellectual curiosity, the more ambiguous and prolonged internal conflict about Black. These are real losses but not fundamental distortions of character.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Harry is the primary agent throughout. He negotiates with Vernon, sneaks to Hogsmeade twice using his own initiative and the Marauder's Map, confronts Lupin about the boggart lesson, asks Lupin to teach him the Patronus, overhears the Three Broomsticks conversation through his own resourcefulness, physically intervenes to stop Black's execution of Pettigrew, blocks Snape, and ultimately casts the Patronus that saves Black and himself. He drives nearly every key plot development through active choice.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Harry's agency is largely preserved. He initiates the Patronus lessons, uses the Marauder's Map independently, confronts Black in the Shrieking Shack, stops Pettigrew's execution, disarms Snape, and casts the decisive Patronus. His role as the central actor is maintained.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Two meaningful agency transfers occur. First, in the Three Broomsticks scene, book Harry overhears the conversation through his own resourcefulness (sneaking in under the Invisibility Cloak via the Honeydukes passage he discovered himself). The film compresses this — Harry is already in Hogsmeade and simply follows Fudge into the pub. The discovery of the passage and the independent planning are absent, reducing the sense that Harry earned this information. Second, the film gives Hermione the howl that saves Harry from the werewolf — in the book, Harry faces the werewolf alone and it is driven off by a second howl from the forest (not Hermione). This is a direct transfer of a dramatic rescue action. Additionally, the book's extended Hogsmeade subplot (two separate trips, the Snape confrontation over the map) is compressed but Harry's agency within it is mostly preserved.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The werewolf rescue transfer is a clear agency loss — in the book Harry faces the werewolf alone and survives through circumstance; in the film Hermione actively saves him. The Three Broomsticks scene reduces Harry's proactive resourcefulness. These are real but not catastrophic losses; Harry remains the primary agent in all major plot beats.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Harry's motivations in PoA are layered. His primary drive is survival and belonging (returning to Hogwarts, escaping the Dursleys). Overlaid on this is a growing obsession with Black — not simply fear but a desire for revenge that he himself cannot fully articulate or resolve. He explicitly does not know whether he wants to kill Black. He is also motivated by loyalty to Hagrid (helping with Buckbeak), by shame (wanting to overcome his dementor weakness), and by a deep, complicated grief for parents he never knew. The internal conflict between wanting to hear his mother's voice again and knowing he must stop is one of the book's most psychologically rich elements.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film simplifies Harry's motivation regarding Black into a cleaner, more dramatic statement: 'I'm going to kill him.' This is more resolved than the book's ambiguity. The shame/weakness motivation around dementors is preserved ('I'm scared, Professor'). The grief for his parents is present but less textured — the film's Patronus memory (imagining his parents talking to him, 'the best I have') is a film invention that replaces the book's more complex process of trying different memories. The Hagrid loyalty motivation is present but compressed.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The key loss is the book's sustained ambiguity about whether Harry wants revenge or justice, and his honest admission that he does not know what he wants. The film resolves this into a clear revenge declaration, then a clear moral choice in the Shack. The internal conflict over hearing his mother's voice — wanting it and knowing he must stop wanting it — is absent from the film. The invented Patronus memory (imagining his parents) replaces the book's more psychologically honest process.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are simplified but not fundamentally wrong. The film Harry still wants to confront Black, still feels shame about dementors, still acts out of loyalty. The loss of the sustained ambiguity and the mother's-voice conflict is a real simplification that flattens psychological depth, but the core motivational structure is intact.

character_arc.book_baseline: Harry's arc in PoA moves from reactive anger and fear (wanting revenge on Black, collapsing before dementors) through gradual mastery (Patronus lessons, growing understanding of his parents) to a moment of genuine moral choice (sparing Pettigrew) and a hard-won but incomplete resolution (Sirius escapes but Harry returns to the Dursleys, the hope of a new home lost). The arc is not triumphant — it ends in loss and renewed isolation, tempered only by the letter from Sirius. A key turning point is Harry's realization that he himself cast the Patronus — that his father 'lives in him' — which reframes his relationship to his parents' memory.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the broad arc: fear and anger → Patronus mastery → moral choice in the Shack → the Patronus revelation → Sirius's escape → return to uncertainty. The 'I saw myself' Patronus realization is present and well-executed. The moral choice to spare Pettigrew is preserved with Harry's explicit reasoning ('I don't reckon my father'd want his best friends to become killers'). The ending — Sirius free but Harry unable to go with him — is present.
character_arc.difference: The arc's emotional texture is compressed. The book's arc includes weeks of dementor shame, the gradual build of the Patronus lessons across multiple sessions, the specific grief of hearing his father's voice for the first time and crying while hiding it, and the extended hope-then-loss of living with Sirius. The film compresses the Patronus training into what appears to be one or two sessions and skips the emotional devastation of hearing his father's voice. The ending is also slightly different in tone — the film ends on the Firebolt flight, which is triumphant, whereas the book ends with Harry on the train, holding Sirius's letter, facing the Dursleys with cautious hope. The film's ending is more upbeat.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's key beats are present and the emotional logic is intact. The compression loses some of the arc's texture and the ending shifts tone slightly toward triumph. These are real losses but the arc is recognizably the same.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Harry's key relationships in PoA: (1) Lupin — mentor/student, built through multiple private sessions, genuine warmth and mutual respect, complicated by Lupin's secrets; (2) Sirius — the revelation of a potential family, moving from hatred to trust to hope; (3) Ron and Hermione — tested by the Firebolt/Scabbers conflict, Harry navigating between them; (4) his parents — present through memory, the dementor voices, and the Patronus revelation.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Lupin relationship is well-preserved — the private lessons, the warmth, the 'your father's son' moment. The Sirius relationship arc (hatred to trust to hope) is intact, including the godfather offer and Harry's eagerness. The Ron/Hermione dynamic is present but compressed. The parents relationship is present through the Patronus memory and the 'I saw my dad' moment.
key_relationships.difference: The Ron/Hermione conflict (Firebolt, Scabbers) is significantly compressed in the film, reducing Harry's role as the uncomfortable middle party navigating between his two best friends — a dynamic that reveals his loyalty and his limits. The Lupin relationship loses some depth from the compression of the Patronus sessions (book has four sessions with distinct emotional beats; film appears to have one or two). The relationship with his parents is slightly altered by the invented Patronus memory.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are mostly faithful in dynamic and function. The compression of the Ron/Hermione conflict reduces one dimension of Harry's relational complexity. No relationship is fundamentally distorted.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book Harry is rich with contradictions: he is brave but ashamed of his weakness; he wants revenge but cannot commit to it; he is strategic (the Vernon negotiation) but also impulsive; he grieves parents he never knew through sounds rather than images; he is capable of cold calculation (the Hogsmeade trips) and of genuine moral restraint (sparing Pettigrew). The book also gives him intellectual curiosity, a sense of humor that emerges under pressure, and a sustained interiority — we see him lying awake, replaying his mother's screams, hiding tears, reasoning through Fudge's odd behavior.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the bravery/shame contradiction, the impulsiveness, the moral restraint, and the humor. It loses most of the interiority — the lying awake, the hidden tears, the sustained reasoning. The strategic coldness (Vernon negotiation) is compressed. The intellectual curiosity is largely absent. The ambiguity about revenge is resolved into cleaner dramatic beats.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant losses: (1) the extended portrait of Harry's self-control under Marge's abuse, which reveals a calculating side rarely seen; (2) the private grief over his mother's voice — the book's most psychologically distinctive material for this installment; (3) the intellectual curiosity and independent reasoning (noticing Fudge's inconsistencies, researching the Grim, working out the Patronus logic); (4) the sustained ambiguity about revenge. The film also adds material not in the books — the invented Patronus memory of his parents talking to him, Sirius's 'trust yourself' speech — which replaces book nuance with more conventional emotional beats.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The losses are real and affect the audience's understanding of Harry's psychological depth. The book Harry is more contradictory, more privately grieving, and more intellectually active than the film Harry. However, the core of his character — the bravery, the moral instinct, the emotional volatility, the loyalty — is preserved. The film is a simplification, not a distortion.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's multi-week strategic self-control under Aunt Marge's abuse, including the calculated leverage over Uncle Vernon for the Hogsmeade form
Harry's private grief over hearing his mother's screams — lying awake in the hospital wing, hiding tears after the Patronus sessions
Harry's sustained ambiguity about whether he wants to kill Black ('he didn't know what he wanted to do')
Multiple Patronus training sessions with distinct emotional beats, including hearing his father's voice for the first time
Harry's intellectual curiosity and independent reasoning (noticing Fudge's odd behavior, researching the Grim, working out the Patronus logic from first principles)
Harry's role as uncomfortable middle party in the Ron/Hermione Firebolt/Scabbers conflict
The werewolf rescue: in the book Harry faces the werewolf alone; in the film Hermione's howl saves him (agency transferred to Hermione)
The book's ending tone — Harry on the train, holding Sirius's letter, facing the Dursleys with cautious hope — replaced by the triumphant Firebolt flight
The invented Patronus memory (imagining his parents talking to him) replaces the book's more psychologically honest process of trying different memories and failing
Sirius's 'trust yourself / find the answers in your heart' speech is a film invention with no book equivalent, replacing more understated book material
score_caps_applied: No hard caps triggered. Score of 79 reflects genuine compression and simplification without fundamental distortion of character. Cap 3 (above 75 if core agency transferred) was considered: the werewolf rescue transfer is real but not a 'core book function' of Harry's character in the way the cap intends — it is one scene, not a defining action. Score kept at 79.
key_observations: The film's Harry Potter in Prisoner of Azkaban is a faithful but simplified version of the book character. The core traits — bravery, emotional volatility, moral instinct, dry humor, stubbornness — are well-preserved, and the major plot-driven character moments (Patronus mastery, sparing Pettigrew, the godfather revelation) are intact. The primary losses are psychological texture: the book's Harry is more privately grieving, more strategically calculating, more intellectually curious, and more genuinely ambiguous about revenge than the film allows. The most distinctive book material — Harry lying awake replaying his mother's screams, hiding tears after hearing his father's voice, the sustained internal conflict about Black — is largely absent. Two agency transfers occur (the werewolf rescue to Hermione; the Three Broomsticks discovery made less proactive), and the film adds invented material (the Patronus memory, Sirius's farewell speech) that replaces book nuance with more conventional emotional beats. The result is a Harry who is recognizably himself but somewhat flattened into cleaner dramatic arcs.
